ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Olmec endonym is unknown; 'Olmec' is a Nahuatl term meaning 'rubber people' applied retroactively by the Aztecs centuries later. The Olmec script remains undeciphered, so all knowledge comes from archaeological evidence. ETHICS-003: The framing of Olmec as 'mother culture of Mesoamerica' oversimplifies complex multi-directional cultural exchange among coeval peoples.

name_variants.name: Olmec
name_variants.context: English academic convention derived from Nahuatl
name_variants.source: Pool, C.A. Olmec Archaeology and Early Mesoamerica (2007)

name_variants.name: Olmeca
name_variants.context: Nahuatl retronym meaning 'rubber people'; not a contemporary self-designation
name_variants.source: Diehl, R.A. The Olmecs: America's First Civilization (2004)

name_variants.name: Xi People
name_variants.context: Controversial and rejected Afrocentric hypothesis by Van Sertima; included for completeness with caveat
name_variants.source: Van Sertima, I. They Came Before Columbus (1976) — rejected by mainstream archaeology

territory_changes.description: Emergence of complex society at San Lorenzo, one of the earliest in Mesoamerica. Monumental architecture and colossal head sculpture indicate centralized authority.

Center of power shifts to La Venta after decline of San Lorenzo. Whether this was political succession or a separate polity is debated. Reasons for San Lorenzo's decline unknown — hypotheses include internal revolt or environmental change.
Decline and abandonment of major Olmec centers. Causes remain debated: environmental change, internal conflict, or shifting trade routes.

sources.citation: Pool, C.A. Olmec Archaeology and Early Mesoamerica (2007), Cambridge University Press
Diehl, R.A. The Olmecs: America's First Civilization (2004), Thames & Hudson
Coe, M.D. & Koontz, R. Mexico: From the Olmecs to the Aztecs (2013), 7th ed., Thames & Hudson

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Maya were never a single unified empire but a network of competing city-states sharing language, writing, and cosmology. 'Kʼuhul Ajaw' (Divine Lord) refers to the title of rulers, not a polity name. ETHICS-002: Spanish conquest involved systematic destruction of Maya codices by Bishop Diego de Landa (1562), destroying incalculable cultural knowledge. Forced conversion and encomienda labor imposed. ETHICS-003: The 'Maya collapse' narrative is oversimplified in Western scholarship; Maya peoples and languages persist today with over 6 million speakers. The Classic Period end was a political reorganization, not an extinction.

name_variants.name: Classic Maya civilization
name_variants.context: Academic periodization for the Classic Period
name_variants.source: Sharer, R.J. & Traxler, L.P. The Ancient Maya (2006)

name_variants.name: Maya
name_variants.context: General English designation covering all periods
name_variants.source: Martin, S. & Grube, N. Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens (2008)

name_variants.name: Civiltà Maya
name_variants.context: Italian academic designation
name_variants.source: Enciclopedia Treccani

territory_changes.description: Earliest settled communities in Maya lowlands. Preclassic period sees development of agriculture, pottery, and early monumental architecture at sites like Nakbe and El Mirador.
The 'Entrada' event: Siyaj Kʼahkʼ, possibly linked to Teotihuacan, conquers Tikal and installs a new dynasty. Evidence of long-distance military-political intervention in Maya politics.

Collapse of Classic Period city-states in the southern lowlands. Not an extinction but a political reorganization. Causes include drought, warfare, deforestation, and social upheaval. Population dispersed rather than disappeared.

Spanish conquest under Pedro de Alvarado and the Montejo family. Destruction of codices by Bishop Diego de Landa (1562) destroyed incalculable cultural knowledge. Forced conversion and encomienda labor system imposed. Disease epidemics devastated populations.

sources.citation: Sharer, R.J. & Traxler, L.P. The Ancient Maya, 6th ed. (2006), Stanford University Press
Martin, S. & Grube, N. Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens (2008), Thames & Hudson
Restall, M. The Maya World: Yucatec Culture and Society, 1550-1850 (1997), Stanford University Press
Clendinnen, I. Ambivalent Conquests: Maya and Spaniard in Yucatan, 1517-1570 (2003), Cambridge University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The original name of this city is unknown; 'Teotihuacan' is the Nahuatl name given centuries later by the Aztecs, meaning 'place where the gods were created.' The ethnic identity and language of its builders remain debated. At its height (c. 450 CE), with 100,000-200,000 inhabitants, it was one of the largest cities in the world. Evidence of human sacrifice is present but its scale and meaning are debated.

name_variants.name: Teotihuacan
name_variants.context: Nahuatl retronym used universally; original name unknown
name_variants.source: Millon, R. Urbanization at Teotihuacan, Mexico (1973)

name_variants.name: City of the Gods
name_variants.context: Literal translation of the Nahuatl name
name_variants.source: Berrin, K. & Pasztory, E. Teotihuacan: Art from the City of the Gods (1993)

territory_changes.description: Rapid urbanization begins as populations from surrounding areas concentrate at Teotihuacan, possibly following volcanic eruptions that displaced nearby Cuicuilco.
Teotihuacan's influence reaches the Maya lowlands. The 'Entrada' at Tikal suggests military or political intervention over vast distances.
Catastrophic burning and deliberate destruction of the city center, likely from internal revolt. Temples were targeted, suggesting political upheaval rather than external invasion.

sources.citation: Millon, R. Urbanization at Teotihuacan, Mexico, Vol. 1 (1973), University of Texas Press
Cowgill, G.L. Ancient Teotihuacan: Early Urbanism in Central Mexico (2015), Cambridge University Press
Sugiyama, S. Human Sacrifice, Militarism, and Rulership (2005), Cambridge University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: The Toltecs occupy a liminal space between history and myth. Much of what is 'known' comes from Aztec accounts that mythologized Toltec rulers (especially Ce Acatl Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl). Archaeological evidence at Tula de Allende confirms a significant urban center, but the extent of a Toltec 'empire' is debated. Care must be taken to distinguish archaeological evidence from later mythological elaboration.

name_variants.name: Toltec Empire
name_variants.context: Common English designation; 'empire' may overstate political unity
name_variants.source: Davies, N. The Toltecs: Until the Fall of Tula (1977)

name_variants.name: Tula
name_variants.context: Modern Spanish name of the archaeological site
name_variants.source: Diehl, R.A. Tula: The Toltec Capital of Ancient Mexico (1983)

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding date. Emergence of Tula as a major center after the collapse of Teotihuacan. The city drew multi-ethnic populations including Nonoalca and Tolteca-Chichimeca groups.
Fall of Tula, attributed in Aztec sources to the exile of Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl and Chichimec invasions. Archaeological evidence shows burning and abandonment.

sources.citation: Davies, N. The Toltecs: Until the Fall of Tula (1977), University of Oklahoma Press
Diehl, R.A. Tula: The Toltec Capital of Ancient Mexico (1983), Thames & Hudson
Sahagún, B. de. Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex), Book 3

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Bēnizàa means 'Cloud People' in Zapotec, the endonym for this civilization. The Zapotec developed one of the earliest writing systems in Mesoamerica. Monte Albán's 'Danzantes' carvings likely depict tortured/sacrificed captives, not dancers as the colonial-era misnomer implies. ETHICS-002: Spanish colonization imposed the encomienda system on surviving populations. Zapotec peoples and language persist today with approximately 500,000 speakers.

name_variants.name: Zapotec civilization
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Marcus, J. & Flannery, K.V. Zapotec Civilization (1996)

name_variants.name: Cloud People
name_variants.context: English translation of the endonym Bēnizàa
name_variants.source: Whitecotton, J.W. The Zapotecs (1977)

territory_changes.description: Foundation of Monte Albán as a hilltop center, possibly as a defensive confederation of valley communities. Development of writing and calendar systems.
Decline of Monte Albán as primary center. Zapotec political power disperses to valley floor sites including Zaachila and Mitla. Not a collapse but a political reorganization.
Spanish conquest. Zapotec communities subjected to encomienda forced labor system and forced conversion. Population devastated by epidemic diseases including smallpox.

sources.citation: Marcus, J. & Flannery, K.V. Zapotec Civilization: How Urban Society Evolved in Mexico's Oaxaca Valley (1996), Thames & Hudson
Whitecotton, J.W. The Zapotecs: Princes, Priests, and Peasants (1977), University of Oklahoma Press
Flannery, K.V. & Marcus, J. The Cloud People (1983), Academic Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Ñuu Dzahui means 'Nation of the Rain' in Mixtec, the endonym for this civilization. 'Mixtec' is an exonym from Nahuatl; the endonym should be preferred. The Mixtec produced extraordinary codices (screenfold books) that are among the few pre-Columbian manuscripts to survive Spanish destruction. These codices document genealogies, conquests, and rituals from the indigenous perspective, making them invaluable primary sources.

name_variants.name: Mixtec kingdom
name_variants.context: English designation using Nahuatl-derived exonym
name_variants.source: Terraciano, K. The Mixtecs of Colonial Oaxaca (2001)

name_variants.name: Nation of the Rain
name_variants.context: English translation of the endonym
name_variants.source: Jansen, M. & Pérez Jiménez, G.A. The Mixtec Pictorial Manuscripts (2011)

territory_changes.description: Early settlement of the Mixteca region. Development of complex society in the highlands of Oaxaca.
Lord 8 Deer Jaguar Claw unifies multiple Mixtec polities through warfare and strategic marriages, documented in Codex Zouche-Nuttall and Codex Bodley. Conquers over 100 communities.
Spanish conquest of Mixtec territories. Pedro de Alvarado subdues Mixtec communities. Encomienda system imposed, forcing indigenous labor in mines and agriculture.

sources.citation: Terraciano, K. The Mixtecs of Colonial Oaxaca: Ñudzahui History, Sixteenth Through Eighteenth Centuries (2001), Stanford University Press
Jansen, M. & Pérez Jiménez, G.A. The Mixtec Pictorial Manuscripts (2011), Brill
Codex Zouche-Nuttall, British Museum

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Muisca are central to the European myth of 'El Dorado,' which was a distortion of the Muisca investiture ceremony at Lake Guatavita into a justification for conquest and plunder. This myth drove devastating Spanish expeditions across South America. ETHICS-002: The Muisca had a sophisticated confederation with two main leaders (Zipa and Zaque) and a complex legal code. The Spanish conquest under Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada destroyed this political structure. Muisca descendants continue to seek recognition of their cultural heritage.

name_variants.name: Muisca Confederation
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Langebaek, C.H. Mercados, poblamiento e integración étnica entre los Muiscas (1987)

name_variants.name: Confederación Muisca
name_variants.context: Spanish academic designation
name_variants.source: Broadbent, S.M. Los Chibchas (1964)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of organized Muisca communities on the high plateau of present-day Colombia. Development of the dual political structure with Zipa (in Bacatá) and Zaque (in Hunza/Tunja).

Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada arrives with Spanish expedition. Zipa Tisquesusa killed. Spanish conquest driven in part by El Dorado legend, a distortion of Muisca ceremonies. Systematic looting of gold offerings from sacred sites.
Final subjugation of the Zaque territory. Muisca political structure dissolved; encomienda system imposed. Population collapse from disease and forced labor.

sources.citation: Langebaek, C.H. Mercados, poblamiento e integración étnica entre los Muiscas, siglo XVI (1987), Banco de la República, Bogotá
Broadbent, S.M. Los Chibchas: organización socio-política (1964), Universidad Nacional de Colombia
Gamboa, J.A. El cacicazgo muisca en los años posteriores a la Conquista (2010), ICANH

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The name 'Wari' comes from the archaeological site; the people's own name for their state is unknown. Evidence suggests both military conquest and diplomatic/economic integration. Wari administrative techniques (including possible quipu usage) may have been precursors to Inca methods. A pre-Inca Andean empire that established administrative centers across a vast territory.

name_variants.name: Wari Empire
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Schreiber, K.J. Wari Imperialism in Middle Horizon Peru (1992)

name_variants.name: Huari
name_variants.context: Spanish-influenced spelling of the site name
name_variants.source: Isbell, W.H. & McEwan, G.F. Huari Administrative Structure (1991)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of Wari as a major urban center in the Ayacucho region. Influenced by both Nazca and Tiwanaku artistic and religious traditions.
Expansion across the Andes from Cusco to Cajamarca. Establishment of provincial administrative centers (e.g., Pikillacta, Viracochapampa) suggests centralized imperial control.
Collapse of the Wari state. Provincial centers abandoned. Drought conditions documented in ice core data may have contributed. Fragmentation into smaller polities.

sources.citation: Schreiber, K.J. Wari Imperialism in Middle Horizon Peru (1992), University of Michigan
Isbell, W.H. & McEwan, G.F. (eds.) Huari Administrative Structure: Prehistoric Monumental Architecture and State Government (1991), Dumbarton Oaks
Jennings, J. (ed.) Beyond Wari Walls: Regional Perspectives on Middle Horizon Peru (2010), University of New Mexico Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Chimú kingdom (self-named Chimor) built Chan Chan, the largest adobe city in the Americas, a UNESCO World Heritage Site now threatened by climate change and erosion. ETHICS-002: The Chimú language (Quingnam/Muchik) is extinct, a direct consequence of Inca and then Spanish conquest that suppressed local languages. Inca conquest involved forced resettlement of Chimú artisans and elites. Evidence of large-scale child sacrifice at Huanchaquito (over 140 children) has been documented archaeologically.

name_variants.name: Chimú Empire
name_variants.context: Common English designation
name_variants.source: Moseley, M.E. The Incas and Their Ancestors (2001)

name_variants.name: Reino Chimú
name_variants.context: Spanish academic designation
name_variants.source: Rowe, J.H. The Kingdom of Chimor (1948)

territory_changes.description: Founding of Chan Chan in the Moche Valley, building on earlier Moche and Lambayeque cultural traditions.
Chimú expansion along 1,000 km of Pacific coastline, incorporating the Lambayeque and other coastal valleys through military conquest.

Inca conquest under Tupac Inca Yupanqui. The Inca diverted rivers to cut off Chan Chan's water supply. Chimú king Minchançaman captured and taken to Cusco. Chimú metalworkers forcibly relocated to serve Inca state.

sources.citation: Moseley, M.E. The Incas and Their Ancestors: The Archaeology of Peru (2001), Thames & Hudson
Rowe, J.H. 'The Kingdom of Chimor,' Acta Americana 6 (1948): 26-59
Prieto, G. et al. 'A mass sacrifice of children and camelids at Huanchaquito-Las Llamas,' PLOS ONE 14(3) (2019)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Moche name is a modern archaeological designation; the people's own name for themselves is unknown. Their language is extinct. The Moche produced extraordinary ceramic art depicting warfare, ritual sacrifice, and sexuality with remarkable realism. Colonial-era biases have historically distorted understanding of Moche ritual practices. Evidence of human sacrifice at Huaca de la Luna is well documented archaeologically.

name_variants.name: Moche civilization
name_variants.context: English academic designation, from the Moche Valley
name_variants.source: Quilter, J. The Moche of Ancient Peru (2010)

name_variants.name: Mochica
name_variants.context: Alternative Spanish designation; also refers to the extinct language
name_variants.source: Bawden, G. The Moche (1996)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of Moche polities along the northern Peruvian coast. Monumental adobe pyramids (Huaca del Sol and Huaca de la Luna) constructed at Cerro Blanco.
Severe El Nino events and drought cause catastrophic flooding and crop failure. Political fragmentation and abandonment of southern Moche centers. Northern centers persist longer.

sources.citation: Quilter, J. The Moche of Ancient Peru: Media and Messages (2010), Peabody Museum Press
Bawden, G. The Moche (1996), Blackwell
Bourget, S. Sex, Death, and Sacrifice in Moche Religion and Visual Culture (2006), University of Texas Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Caral is the oldest known urban center in the Americas, contemporaneous with ancient Egyptian pyramids. The original name is unknown; 'Caral' is the modern Quechua name. ETHICS-003: Remarkably, no evidence of warfare, weapons, or fortifications has been found, challenging narratives that assume complex societies require militarism. The site was nearly destroyed by modern development before archaeological recognition.

name_variants.name: Caral-Supe civilization
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Shady Solís, R. La Ciudad Sagrada de Caral-Supe (2003)

name_variants.name: Norte Chico civilization
name_variants.context: Alternative English designation used in some North American literature
name_variants.source: Haas, J. & Creamer, W. 'Crucible of Andean Civilization,' Current Anthropology (2006)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of monumental architecture at Caral and neighboring sites. Cotton cultivation and marine fishing form economic base. No evidence of ceramics, writing, or warfare — a unique development model.
Abandonment of Caral and associated sites. Causes unknown but may relate to environmental changes including earthquakes and El Nino events.

sources.citation: Shady Solís, R. La Ciudad Sagrada de Caral-Supe: los orígenes de la civilización andina (2003), INC, Lima
Haas, J. & Creamer, W. 'Crucible of Andean Civilization,' Current Anthropology 47(5) (2006): 745-775
Shady Solís, R. et al. 'Dating Caral, a Preceramic Site in the Supe Valley,' Science 292 (2001): 723-726

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The endonym for this state is uncertain; 'Tiwanaku' may derive from Aymara. The site was heavily looted during the colonial period and stones were used for church construction. Inca and later Spanish accounts mythologized the site. ETHICS-003: Modern Bolivia has adopted Tiwanaku iconography as a national symbol, raising questions about whose heritage it represents given the complex ethnic history of the altiplano.

name_variants.name: Tiahuanaco
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial-era spelling, still used in some literature
name_variants.source: Kolata, A.L. The Tiwanaku (1993)

name_variants.name: Tiwanaku Empire
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Janusek, J.W. Ancient Tiwanaku (2008)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of Tiwanaku as a major ceremonial and urban center at over 3,800m elevation. Development of raised-field agriculture (suka kollus) to farm the high-altitude altiplano.

Maximum extent of Tiwanaku influence from southern Peru to northern Chile and northwest Argentina. Expansion combined colonization, trade networks, and religious prestige rather than purely military conquest.
Collapse of Tiwanaku state following prolonged drought documented in ice core and lake sediment data. Population dispersed into competing Aymara kingdoms (señoríos).

sources.citation: Kolata, A.L. The Tiwanaku: Portrait of an Andean Civilization (1993), Blackwell
Janusek, J.W. Ancient Tiwanaku (2008), Cambridge University Press
Stanish, C. Ancient Titicaca (2003), University of California Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Purépecha (also called Tarascan, an exonym possibly of Spanish origin) maintained independence from the Aztec Empire, one of the few Mesoamerican states to resist Aztec expansion. Their language is a language isolate with no known relatives. ETHICS-002: Spanish conquest under Nuño de Guzmán was particularly brutal — Guzmán tortured and executed the last cazonci (ruler) Tangáxuan II and was recalled to Spain for his atrocities. The Relación de Michoacán (1541) is a crucial indigenous source written shortly after conquest.

name_variants.name: Tarascan State
name_variants.context: English designation using the exonym 'Tarascan'
name_variants.source: Pollard, H.P. Tariacuri's Legacy (1993)

name_variants.name: Purépecha Empire
name_variants.context: Using the endonym; increasingly preferred in scholarship
name_variants.source: Roskamp, H. La historiografía indígena de Michoacán (1998)

name_variants.name: Reino Purépecha
name_variants.context: Spanish designation using the endonym
name_variants.source: Relación de Michoacán (1541)

territory_changes.description: Consolidation of the Purépecha state under the legendary leader Tariacuri. Three-part political structure centered on Tzintzuntzan, Ihuatzio, and Pátzcuaro.
Purépecha decisively defeat a massive Aztec invasion, maintaining their independence. This demonstrates that the Aztec Empire was not invincible and faced effective resistance.

Nuño de Guzmán's conquest of Michoacán. Torture and execution of Cazonci Tangáxuan II despite his initial cooperation with the Spanish. Guzmán's brutality was extreme even by the standards of the conquest and he was later tried for his crimes.

sources.citation: Pollard, H.P. Tariacuri's Legacy: The Prehispanic Tarascan State (1993), University of Oklahoma Press
Relación de Michoacán (c. 1541), trans. Craine, E.R. & Reindorp, R.C. (1970)
Roskamp, H. La historiografía indígena de Michoacán (1998), CNWS, Leiden

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Previously known as 'Anasazi,' a Navajo word sometimes interpreted as 'ancient enemies.' The term is rejected by many Pueblo peoples as disrespectful, hence the shift to 'Ancestral Puebloans' in academic usage. However, this term is also imperfect as it implies a direct linear ancestry that oversimplifies complex migrations. ETHICS-003: The 'mystery of the disappearance' narrative is misleading; the people migrated to the Rio Grande, Zuni, and Hopi areas — they did not vanish. Modern Pueblo peoples maintain oral traditions about these ancestors.

name_variants.name: Anasazi
name_variants.context: Navajo term historically used in archaeology; considered disrespectful by many Pueblo peoples
name_variants.source: Lekson, S.H. The Chaco Meridian (1999)

name_variants.name: Ancestral Puebloans
name_variants.context: Preferred modern academic term
name_variants.source: Noble, D.G. (ed.) In Search of Chaco (2004)

territory_changes.region: Four Corners region (Colorado, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico)
territory_changes.description: Development of settled agricultural communities in the Four Corners region. Basketmaker II and III periods show increasing architectural complexity.

Chaco Canyon emerges as a major regional center with Great Houses (Pueblo Bonito, Chetro Ketl) and an extensive road network. Whether this represents political centralization or ritual pilgrimage is debated.

Migration away from the Four Corners region following prolonged drought (documented in tree-ring data). Populations moved south and east. Not a 'disappearance' but a reorganization; descendants are modern Pueblo peoples (Hopi, Zuni, Acoma, and others).

sources.citation: Lekson, S.H. The Chaco Meridian (1999), AltaMira Press
Noble, D.G. (ed.) In Search of Chaco (2004), School of American Research Press
Cordell, L.S. & McBrinn, M.E. Archaeology of the Southwest (2012), 3rd ed., Left Coast Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Nueva España was built on the destruction of indigenous civilizations. The encomienda and repartimiento systems constituted forced labor regimes. The population of central Mexico collapsed from an estimated 25 million in 1519 to approximately 1 million by 1620 — one of the greatest demographic catastrophes in human history, caused by European diseases (smallpox, measles, typhus) combined with exploitation and social disruption. The casta system institutionalized racial hierarchy. Silver mining relied on devastating forced indigenous labor (mita). Indigenous languages, religions, and cultural practices were systematically suppressed.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of New Spain
name_variants.context: English designation for the viceregal period
name_variants.source: Bakewell, P. A History of Latin America (2004)

name_variants.name: Virreinato de Nueva España
name_variants.context: Full official Spanish title after creation of viceroyalty
name_variants.source: Gibson, C. The Aztecs Under Spanish Rule (1964)

territory_changes.description: Fall of Tenochtitlan to Hernán Cortés and indigenous allies (especially Tlaxcala). Smallpox epidemic devastated defenders. The city was razed and Mexico City built on its ruins — a deliberate act of cultural erasure.

Formal establishment of the Viceroyalty with Antonio de Mendoza as first viceroy. Encomienda system formalized. Territory extended from present-day western USA to Central America and across the Pacific to the Philippines.
Mexican War of Independence (1810-1821). Plan de Iguala and Treaty of Córdoba establish independence. Led initially by Miguel Hidalgo (1810) and José María Morelos, completed by Agustín de Iturbide.

sources.citation: Gibson, C. The Aztecs Under Spanish Rule (1964), Stanford University Press
Bakewell, P. A History of Latin America (2004), Blackwell
Cook, S.F. & Borah, W.W. The Indian Population of Central Mexico, 1531-1610 (1960), University of California Press
León-Portilla, M. Visión de los vencidos: relaciones indígenas de la conquista (1959), UNAM

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Built on the destruction of the Inca Empire and the exploitation of indigenous peoples through the mita system (forced labor, especially in the silver mines of Potosi where millions died). Mercury amalgamation process used in mining caused mass poisoning. Encomienda system reduced indigenous peoples to near-slavery. Tupac Amaru II's rebellion (1780-1781) was a major indigenous uprising, brutally suppressed — the rebel leader publicly tortured and dismembered. Population of the Inca realm estimated at 10-12 million in 1520 collapsed to approximately 600,000 by 1620.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of Peru
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Andrien, K.J. Crisis and Decline (1985)

territory_changes.description: Creation of the Viceroyalty by the New Laws, initially encompassing most of Spanish-controlled South America. Built on the conquest of the Inca Empire (1532-1572). Indigenous populations subjected to mita forced labor, particularly devastating in the Potosi silver mines.
Execution of Tupac Amaru, the last Sapa Inca of the Neo-Inca state at Vilcabamba, by Viceroy Francisco de Toledo. Marks the final destruction of Inca political sovereignty.

Suppression of the Tupac Amaru II rebellion — the largest indigenous uprising in colonial history. José Gabriel Condorcanqui publicly dismembered in Cusco. The rebellion involved over 100,000 indigenous participants.
Battle of Ayacucho ends Spanish rule in South America. Independence achieved through campaigns of Simón Bolívar and Antonio José de Sucre.

sources.citation: Andrien, K.J. Crisis and Decline: The Viceroyalty of Peru in the Seventeenth Century (1985), University of New Mexico Press
Stern, S.J. Peru's Indian Peoples and the Challenge of Spanish Conquest (1993), University of Wisconsin Press
Walker, C.F. The Tupac Amaru Rebellion (2014), Harvard University Press
Robins, N. Mercury, Mining, and Empire (2011), Indiana University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Colonial Brazil was the largest single destination of the transatlantic slave trade, receiving approximately 4.9 million enslaved Africans — more than any other country in the Americas. The sugar plantation (engenho) system was built entirely on enslaved African labor. Indigenous populations were also enslaved through bandeirante expeditions until Pombal's reforms (1755-1758). The indigenous population, estimated at 2-4 million at contact, was devastated by disease, slavery, and displacement. Brazil was the last country in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery (1888).

name_variants.name: Colonial Brazil
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Schwartz, S.B. Sugar Plantations in the Formation of Brazilian Society (1985)

name_variants.name: Terra de Santa Cruz
name_variants.context: Original Portuguese name before 'Brazil' became standard
name_variants.source: Hemming, J. Red Gold (1978)

territory_changes.description: Pedro Álvares Cabral claims the territory for Portugal. Initial colonization focuses on brazilwood extraction using indigenous forced labor.

Establishment of sugar plantation economy. Mass importation of enslaved Africans begins. Over the colonial period, approximately 4.9 million enslaved people were transported from Africa to Brazil — the largest forced migration to any single colony in history.

Pedro I declares Brazilian independence. Unlike Spanish American independence, achieved with minimal violence. However, slavery continued for another 66 years; the plantation economy and racial hierarchy persisted.

sources.citation: Schwartz, S.B. Sugar Plantations in the Formation of Brazilian Society: Bahia, 1550-1835 (1985), Cambridge University Press
Hemming, J. Red Gold: The Conquest of the Brazilian Indians (1978), Harvard University Press
Klein, H.S. & Luna, F.V. Slavery in Brazil (2010), Cambridge University Press
Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, slavevoyages.org

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Established on land taken from indigenous peoples through coerced or fraudulent treaties, warfare, and displacement. The colonies became major participants in the Atlantic slave trade; by 1776, approximately 500,000 enslaved Africans lived in the colonies, forming the economic foundation of the Southern colonies. The Declaration of Independence (1776) proclaimed 'all men are created equal' while excluding enslaved people, indigenous peoples, and women. ETHICS-003: Colonization involved the systematic dispossession of indigenous nations including the Powhatan, Wampanoag, Lenape, and many others.

name_variants.name: British America
name_variants.context: Broader designation including the thirteen plus other British colonies
name_variants.source: Taylor, A. American Colonies (2001)

name_variants.name: Tredici Colonie
name_variants.context: Italian designation
name_variants.source: Enciclopedia Treccani

territory_changes.description: Founding of Jamestown on Powhatan territory. Immediate conflict with indigenous Powhatan Confederacy. Introduction of tobacco cultivation and, from 1619, enslaved African labor.

Pilgrims found Plymouth Colony on Wampanoag land. Benefited from the massive indigenous population collapse of 1616-1619 (possibly leptospirosis or hepatitis) that killed up to 90% of coastal New England peoples.
Declaration of Independence from Great Britain. The new republic was founded with the contradiction of declaring universal rights while maintaining slavery and continuing indigenous dispossession.

sources.citation: Taylor, A. American Colonies: The Settling of North America (2001), Penguin
Berlin, I. Many Thousands Gone: The First Two Centuries of Slavery in North America (1998), Harvard University Press
Richter, D.K. Facing East from Indian Country (2001), Harvard University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Haiti was the first successful slave revolution in history and the first Black republic (1804). France's Saint-Domingue was the most profitable slave colony in history, where enslaved people were literally worked to death (average life expectancy on sugar plantations was 7 years after arrival). ETHICS-003: After independence, France extorted 150 million francs (later reduced to 90 million) in 1825 as 'indemnity' for lost slave property, forcing Haiti to pay for its own freedom. This debt, not fully repaid until 1947, crippled Haiti's economy for over a century. The USA did not recognize Haiti until 1862. Western historiography has systematically undervalued the Haitian Revolution's significance.

name_variants.name: Republic of Haiti
name_variants.context: English designation
name_variants.source: Dubois, L. Haiti: The Aftershocks of History (2012)

name_variants.name: Saint-Domingue
name_variants.context: French colonial name before independence
name_variants.source: James, C.L.R. The Black Jacobins (1938)

name_variants.name: Ayiti
name_variants.context: Haitian Creole name, from the Taino word for 'land of high mountains'
name_variants.source: Trouillot, M.-R. Silencing the Past (1995)

territory_changes.description: Enslaved people revolt at Bois Caiman ceremony. Beginning of the Haitian Revolution, led by Toussaint Louverture, Jean-Jacques Dessalines, and Henri Christophe. The only successful large-scale slave revolt in history.

Jean-Jacques Dessalines declares independence on January 1, 1804, using the Taino name 'Hayti.' The first free Black republic and the second independent nation in the Western Hemisphere. Dessalines orders massacre of remaining French colonists.

France extorts 150 million gold francs (later reduced to 90 million) as 'indemnity' for lost slave property, under threat of military invasion. Haiti borrows from French banks at exploitative rates. This debt, not repaid until 1947, is a foundational cause of Haiti's economic underdevelopment.

sources.citation: James, C.L.R. The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (1938), Vintage
Dubois, L. Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution (2004), Harvard University Press
Trouillot, M.-R. Silencing the Past: Power and the Production of History (1995), Beacon Press
Fick, C. The Making of Haiti (1990), University of Tennessee Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: The First Mexican Empire under Agustín de Iturbide was a brief monarchical experiment following independence from Spain. While independence ended formal colonial rule, the social and racial hierarchies of the colonial casta system largely persisted. Indigenous and mixed-race populations remained marginalized. The vast territory claimed (from Oregon to Central America) was largely nominal and quickly fragmented.

name_variants.name: First Mexican Empire
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Anna, T.E. The Mexican Empire of Iturbide (1990)

name_variants.name: Imperio de Iturbide
name_variants.context: Informal designation referencing the emperor
name_variants.source: Robertson, W.S. Iturbide of Mexico (1952)

territory_changes.description: Agustín de Iturbide crowned Emperor after the Plan de Iguala united royalists and insurgents. Territory included present-day Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and parts of the USA.

Central American provinces secede to form the Federal Republic of Central America. Iturbide abdicates under pressure from republican forces led by Antonio López de Santa Anna. Mexico becomes a republic.

sources.citation: Anna, T.E. The Mexican Empire of Iturbide (1990), University of Nebraska Press
Robertson, W.S. Iturbide of Mexico (1952), Duke University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: The Confederate States of America was explicitly founded to preserve and expand race-based chattel slavery. Vice President Alexander Stephens stated in his 'Cornerstone Speech' (1861) that the Confederacy rested 'upon the great truth that the negro is not equal to the white man; that slavery subordination to the superior race is his natural and normal condition.' Multiple state secession declarations explicitly cite slavery as the primary cause. Post-war 'Lost Cause' mythology attempted to reframe the war as about 'states' rights,' but the historical record is unambiguous: it was about the right to own human beings. Approximately 4 million people were enslaved in the Confederate states.

name_variants.name: CSA
name_variants.context: Common abbreviation
name_variants.source: McPherson, J.M. Battle Cry of Freedom (1988)

name_variants.name: The Confederacy
name_variants.context: Informal designation
name_variants.source: Davis, W.C. Look Away! (2002)

name_variants.name: Stati Confederati d'America
name_variants.context: Italian designation
name_variants.source: Enciclopedia Treccani

territory_changes.description: Eleven slaveholding states secede from the United States following the election of Abraham Lincoln. Founded explicitly to preserve race-based chattel slavery, as documented in the secession declarations of South Carolina, Mississippi, Georgia, and Texas.

Military defeat by the United States. Approximately 750,000 soldiers died in the Civil War. Emancipation of approximately 4 million enslaved people. Reconstruction era attempts at racial equality were subsequently undermined by Jim Crow laws.

sources.citation: McPherson, J.M. Battle Cry of Freedom: The Civil War Era (1988), Oxford University Press
Stephens, A.H. 'Cornerstone Speech,' March 21, 1861, Savannah, Georgia
Davis, W.C. Look Away! A History of the Confederate States of America (2002), Free Press
Declarations of Causes of Secession (SC, MS, GA, TX, VA), 1860-1861

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Viceroyalty of Nueva Granada encompassed present-day Colombia, Ecuador, Venezuela, and Panama. It was built on the subjugation of indigenous peoples (including the Muisca) and the importation of enslaved Africans, particularly for gold mining in the Chocó and plantation agriculture. Cartagena de Indias was one of the major slave ports of the Americas. Indigenous peoples were subjected to the mita system of forced labor. The Comunero Revolt of 1781 was an early expression of resistance to colonial exploitation.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of New Granada
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: McFarlane, A. Colombia Before Independence (1993)

name_variants.name: Nuevo Reino de Granada
name_variants.context: Name of the territory before the viceroyalty was created
name_variants.source: Kuethe, A.J. Military Reform and Society in New Granada (1978)

territory_changes.description: Creation of the Viceroyalty of Nueva Granada, separated from the Viceroyalty of Peru. Initially suppressed in 1723 and re-established permanently in 1739. Territory encompassed present-day Colombia, Ecuador, Venezuela, and Panama.
Battle of Boyacá (August 7, 1819) secures independence. Simón Bolívar's forces defeat the Spanish. The viceroyalty is dissolved and succeeded by Gran Colombia.

sources.citation: McFarlane, A. Colombia Before Independence (1993), Cambridge University Press
Kuethe, A.J. Military Reform and Society in New Granada, 1773-1808 (1978), University of Florida Press

name_original: Virreinato del Río de la Plata
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The last major Spanish colonial administrative unit created in the Americas. While it had a smaller enslaved population than Brazil or the Caribbean, slavery existed in Buenos Aires and the interior. The territory encompassed indigenous lands of the Guaraní, Mapuche, Pampa, and Patagonian peoples who were progressively dispossessed. The Bourbon Reforms that created the viceroyalty intensified exploitation of the Potosi silver mines.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Rock, D. Argentina 1516-1987 (1987)

territory_changes.description: Creation of the Viceroyalty from territories previously under the Viceroyalty of Peru. Encompassed present-day Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, and parts of southern Brazil.
May Revolution (Revolución de Mayo): creation of the Primera Junta in Buenos Aires. Beginning of the Argentine War of Independence. The viceregal structure collapses; various successor states emerge.

sources.citation: Rock, D. Argentina 1516-1987 (1987), University of California Press
Lynch, J. Spanish Colonial Administration, 1782-1810 (1958), Athlone Press

capital_name: Santiago de los Caballeros de Guatemala
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Captaincy General of Guatemala encompassed all of Central America. Indigenous Maya peoples were subjected to forced labor through repartimiento and encomienda systems. Cochineal and indigo production relied on exploited indigenous labor. The colonial-era racial hierarchy (sistema de castas) persisted long after independence. Maya peoples constituted the majority of the population but were systematically marginalized. The legacy of colonial land distribution continues to drive inequality in Guatemala and Central America.

name_variants.name: Captaincy General of Guatemala
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Lutz, C.H. Santiago de Guatemala, 1541-1773 (1994)

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Guatemala
name_variants.context: Alternative designation reflecting its semi-autonomous status
name_variants.source: Wortman, M.L. Government and Society in Central America (1982)

territory_changes.description: Formal establishment of the Captaincy General as a largely autonomous unit within the Viceroyalty of New Spain. Territory encompassed present-day Guatemala, Belize, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Chiapas.
Declaration of independence on September 15, 1821. Briefly annexed to the Mexican Empire (1822-1823), then formed the Federal Republic of Central America before fragmenting into individual nations.

sources.citation: Lutz, C.H. Santiago de Guatemala, 1541-1773 (1994), University of Oklahoma Press
Wortman, M.L. Government and Society in Central America, 1680-1840 (1982), Columbia University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Notable for the Arauco War (1536-1883), one of the longest conflicts in the Americas, in which the Mapuche people successfully resisted Spanish colonization south of the Biobío River for over 300 years. The Treaty of Quillin (1641) recognized Mapuche sovereignty — one of the few instances where a European colonial power formally recognized indigenous sovereignty. The encomienda system was imposed on indigenous peoples in the conquered territories north of the Biobío.

name_variants.name: Captaincy General of Chile
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Collier, S. & Sater, W.F. A History of Chile (2004)

territory_changes.description: Pedro de Valdivia founds Santiago on Mapuche and Picunche territory. Beginning of the Arauco War, the longest continuous military resistance to European colonization in the Americas.
Treaty of Quillin: Spain formally recognizes Mapuche sovereignty south of the Biobío River. One of the most significant recognitions of indigenous sovereignty in colonial history.
Chilean independence declared February 12, 1818, following the Battle of Chacabuco (1817) and the campaigns of Bernardo O'Higgins and José de San Martín.

sources.citation: Collier, S. & Sater, W.F. A History of Chile, 1808-2002 (2004), Cambridge University Press
Bengoa, J. Historia del pueblo mapuche: siglos XIX y XX (1985), Ediciones Sur
Ercilla y Zúñiga, A. de. La Araucana (1569-1589)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Hamalainen's revisionist scholarship challenges the narrative that Native peoples were merely victims of European expansion, showing the Comanche as empire-builders who dominated trade, diplomacy, and warfare across a vast region. However, the Comanche also engaged in slave-raiding and captive-taking. ETHICS-002: Their defeat came through deliberate US Army campaigns of buffalo extermination (destroying the economic base) and forced relocation to reservations. The Comanche had no fixed capital; 'Nʉmʉnʉʉ' is the endonym meaning 'the people.'

name_variants.name: Comanche Empire
name_variants.context: Term popularized by Hamalainen (2008); challenges assumptions about what constitutes an empire
name_variants.source: Hamalainen, P. The Comanche Empire (2008)

name_variants.name: Nʉmʉnʉʉ
name_variants.context: Comanche endonym meaning 'the people'
name_variants.source: Kavanagh, T.W. Comanche Political History (1996)

territory_changes.description: Comanche bands expand southward from the Rocky Mountains, displacing Apache peoples. Adoption of the horse transforms Comanche military capability and economy.

Comancheria reaches maximum extent, dominating trade and diplomacy across the southern Plains. The Comanche effectively control Spanish access to the interior, forcing treaties on European colonial powers.

Quanah Parker and the last free Comanche bands surrender after the Red River War (1874-1875). US Army strategy of buffalo extermination deliberately destroyed the Comanche way of life. Forced relocation to reservations in Indian Territory (Oklahoma).

sources.citation: Hamalainen, P. The Comanche Empire (2008), Yale University Press
Kavanagh, T.W. Comanche Political History (1996), University of Nebraska Press
Hagan, W.T. United States-Comanche Relations (1976), Yale University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Sioux' is a French corruption of an Ojibwe exonym sometimes considered pejorative; the endonym 'Lakȟóta' should be preferred. ETHICS-002: The US government's response to Lakota resistance included targeted destruction of buffalo herds (40-60 million bison reduced to near extinction), the forcible taking of the Black Hills (Paha Sapa) in violation of the Fort Laramie Treaty of 1868, and the Wounded Knee Massacre (1890) where US soldiers killed approximately 250-300 Lakota men, women, and children. The US Supreme Court ruled in 1980 that the taking of the Black Hills was illegal; the Lakota have refused monetary compensation, demanding land return.

name_variants.name: Lakota Sioux
name_variants.context: Common English designation; 'Sioux' is a French corruption of an Ojibwe exonym
name_variants.source: Ostler, J. The Lakotas and the Black Hills (2010)

name_variants.name: Teton Sioux
name_variants.context: Historical English designation; 'Teton' from Thítȟuŋwaŋ (dwellers of the prairie)
name_variants.source: DeMallie, R.J. (ed.) Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 13 (2001)

name_variants.name: Očhéthi Šakówiŋ
name_variants.context: The broader 'Seven Council Fires' alliance of Sioux peoples to which the Lakota belong
name_variants.source: DeMallie, R.J. (ed.) Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 13 (2001)

territory_changes.description: Lakota bands are part of the broader Oceti Sakowin (Seven Council Fires) alliance, centered in the Great Lakes region.
Lakota expansion westward onto the Great Plains, facilitated by the horse. The Black Hills (Paha Sapa) become the sacred center of Lakota territory.

Fort Laramie Treaty recognizes the Great Sioux Reservation including the Black Hills. The US agrees to abandon forts along the Bozeman Trail after Red Cloud's War. Treaty subsequently violated by the US government.

US Army seizes the Black Hills following Custer's defeat at Little Bighorn (1876). Forced removal to diminished reservations. Buffalo herds systematically destroyed. The 1980 Supreme Court ruling confirmed this seizure was illegal, awarding $105 million that the Lakota have refused, demanding land return.

sources.citation: Ostler, J. The Lakotas and the Black Hills (2010), Viking
DeMallie, R.J. (ed.) Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 13: Plains (2001), Smithsonian Institution
United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians, 448 U.S. 371 (1980)
Dunbar-Ortiz, R. An Indigenous Peoples' History of the United States (2014), Beacon Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Cherokee Nation exemplifies the devastation of US Indian removal policy. Despite adopting Western institutions (written constitution, newspaper in Sequoyah's syllabary, settled agriculture), the Cherokee were forcibly removed under the Indian Removal Act of 1830. The Trail of Tears (1838-1839) resulted in approximately 4,000-8,000 deaths during forced marches. This occurred despite the Supreme Court ruling in Worcester v. Georgia (1832) that Cherokee sovereignty was legally valid. ETHICS-003: The Cherokee Nation persists today as a federally recognized sovereign nation.

name_variants.name: Cherokee Nation
name_variants.context: English designation; the Cherokee Nation continues as a sovereign entity today
name_variants.source: Perdue, T. & Green, M.D. The Cherokee Nation and the Trail of Tears (2007)

name_variants.name: Tsalagi
name_variants.context: Cherokee endonym in Latin script transliteration
name_variants.source: Mooney, J. Myths of the Cherokee (1900)

territory_changes.description: Earliest European contact with the Cherokee during Hernando de Soto's expedition. Cherokee territory encompassed approximately 140,000 square miles across the southeastern United States.

Trail of Tears: forced removal of approximately 16,000 Cherokee from their ancestral lands to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Approximately 4,000-8,000 died during the march from exposure, disease, and starvation. This occurred in defiance of the Supreme Court ruling in Worcester v. Georgia (1832).

Oklahoma statehood dissolves the formal Cherokee Nation government. The Curtis Act (1898) and Dawes Act had already dismantled communal land ownership, allotting individual plots and opening 'surplus' land to white settlement. Cherokee Nation government reconstituted in the 1970s.

sources.citation: Perdue, T. & Green, M.D. The Cherokee Nation and the Trail of Tears (2007), Viking
McLoughlin, W.G. Cherokee Renascence in the New Republic (1986), Princeton University Press
Worcester v. Georgia, 31 U.S. (6 Pet.) 515 (1832)
Mooney, J. Myths of the Cherokee, Bureau of American Ethnology, 19th Annual Report (1900)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Creek' is an English colonial exonym derived from the waterways in their territory; the endonym 'Mvskoke' should be preferred. ETHICS-002: Like the Cherokee, the Muscogee were subjected to forced removal under the Indian Removal Act. The Creek War (1813-1814) resulted in the loss of 23 million acres. Andrew Jackson, aided by Creek allies (Lower Creeks), then also subjected those allies to removal — a betrayal of alliance. ETHICS-003: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation persists today as a federally recognized sovereign nation in Oklahoma. The confederacy had no single capital; it was organized around tribal towns (talwa).

name_variants.name: Creek Confederacy
name_variants.context: English colonial exonym derived from the waterways in their territory
name_variants.source: Ethridge, R. Creek Country (2003)

name_variants.name: Muscogee (Creek) Nation
name_variants.context: Current official designation; nation continues as a sovereign entity
name_variants.source: Green, M.D. The Politics of Indian Removal (1982)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of the Creek Confederacy as a multi-ethnic political alliance organized around autonomous tribal towns (talwa). Territory spanned much of present-day Alabama and Georgia.

Treaty of Fort Jackson: after the Creek War, Andrew Jackson forces the cession of 23 million acres, including from Creek allies who had fought alongside the US. Widely considered one of the most unjust treaties in US history.

Treaty of Cusseta and subsequent forced removal to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Thousands died during removal marches. Creek lands taken through fraud, intimidation, and military force despite treaty guarantees.

sources.citation: Ethridge, R. Creek Country: The Creek Indians and Their World (2003), University of North Carolina Press
Green, M.D. The Politics of Indian Removal (1982), University of Nebraska Press
Saunt, C. A New Order of Things (1999), Cambridge University Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Huron' is a French exonym possibly meaning 'boar's head' or 'ruffian'; the endonym 'Wendat' should be preferred. ETHICS-002: The Wendat were devastated by European diseases (particularly smallpox in the 1630s-1640s) and the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Beaver Wars, reducing their population from approximately 20,000-40,000 to a few hundred survivors. Jesuit missionary records, while valuable as historical sources, also represent the project of cultural and religious destruction. ETHICS-003: Wendat descendants persist today as the Wyandotte Nation (Oklahoma) and the Wendat Nation (Wendake, Quebec).

name_variants.name: Huron Confederacy
name_variants.context: English designation using French exonym; considered disrespectful by some Wendat
name_variants.source: Trigger, B.G. The Children of Aataentsic (1976)

name_variants.name: Confédération huronne
name_variants.context: French colonial designation
name_variants.source: Thwaites, R.G. (ed.) The Jesuit Relations (1896-1901)

territory_changes.description: Consolidation of the Wendat Confederacy, comprising four to five nations. Agricultural society practicing longhouse living with democratic decision-making.

Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) attacks destroy Wendat villages in the Beaver Wars, driven by competition over the fur trade with European powers. Population already devastated by smallpox epidemics of 1634-1640. Survivors scatter to various refuges.

sources.citation: Trigger, B.G. The Children of Aataentsic: A History of the Huron People to 1660 (1976), McGill-Queen's University Press
Thwaites, R.G. (ed.) The Jesuit Relations and Allied Documents, 73 vols. (1896-1901)
Sioui, G.E. Huron-Wendat: The Heritage of the Circle (1999), UBC Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: The Powhatan Confederacy (self-named Tsenacommacah) is known primarily through the Jamestown encounter. ETHICS-003: The dominant Pocahontas narrative has been heavily romanticized and distorted. Pocahontas (Amonute/Matoaka) was kidnapped, held hostage, converted, and married to John Rolfe as a political tool — the 'love story' obscures colonial violence. Wahunsenacah (Chief Powhatan) built a complex political alliance of approximately 30 tributary peoples. Powhatan descendants continue to live in Virginia; the Pamunkey and Mattaponi reservations are the oldest in the United States.

name_variants.name: Powhatan Confederacy
name_variants.context: English designation from the paramount chief's name/title
name_variants.source: Rountree, H.C. Pocahontas, Powhatan, Opechancanough (2005)

name_variants.name: Powhatan Chiefdom
name_variants.context: Academic designation emphasizing paramount chief structure
name_variants.source: Gleach, F.W. Powhatan's World and Colonial Virginia (1997)

territory_changes.description: Wahunsenacah (Powhatan) consolidates approximately 30 Algonquian-speaking peoples into a confederacy. A combination of military force, diplomacy, and inheritance.
Opechancanough leads a coordinated attack on English settlements, killing approximately 350 colonists (a quarter of the population). English retaliatory campaigns devastate Powhatan communities.
Treaty of Middle Plantation after Bacon's Rebellion. Surviving Powhatan groups confined to small reservations. Effective end of Powhatan political sovereignty, though communities persist to this day.

sources.citation: Rountree, H.C. Pocahontas, Powhatan, Opechancanough (2005), University of Virginia Press
Gleach, F.W. Powhatan's World and Colonial Virginia (1997), University of Nebraska Press
Smith, J. The Generall Historie of Virginia, New-England, and the Summer Isles (1624)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Wampanoag ('People of the First Light') have inhabited southeastern New England for at least 12,000 years. Their encounter with the Pilgrims at Plymouth (1620) has been mythologized in the American Thanksgiving narrative, which obscures the reality: the Wampanoag were devastated by a European-introduced epidemic (1616-1619) killing approximately 90% of the population before the Pilgrims arrived. Massasoit's alliance was strategic, not naive generosity. King Philip's War (1675-1676) was one of the deadliest per capita in American history. Metacom's head was displayed on a pike at Plymouth for 20 years. ETHICS-003: The Wampanoag persist today as the Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe and the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah).

name_variants.name: Wampanoag
name_variants.context: Standard English designation
name_variants.source: Silverman, D.J. This Land Is Their Land (2019)

name_variants.name: People of the First Light
name_variants.context: Translation of the endonym
name_variants.source: Brooks, L. Our Beloved Kin (2018)

territory_changes.description: European-introduced epidemic (possibly leptospirosis or hepatitis) kills an estimated 75-90% of the Wampanoag population before sustained English colonization. This catastrophe fundamentally altered the power dynamics the Pilgrims encountered.

English Pilgrims establish Plymouth Colony on the site of the depopulated Wampanoag village of Patuxet. Massasoit (Ousamequin) forms strategic alliance with the colonists against the Narragansett — political calculation, not naivety.

End of King Philip's War (Metacom's War), the deadliest per capita war in American history. Metacom killed; his head displayed on a pike at Plymouth for 20 years. His wife and son sold into slavery in the Caribbean. Wampanoag sovereignty effectively destroyed.

sources.citation: Silverman, D.J. This Land Is Their Land: The Wampanoag Indians, Plymouth Colony, and the Troubled History of Thanksgiving (2019), Bloomsbury
Brooks, L. Our Beloved Kin: A New History of King Philip's War (2018), Yale University Press
Lepore, J. The Name of War (1998), Knopf

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Pueblo' is a Spanish exonym meaning 'village/people,' applied to diverse indigenous groups (Hopi, Zuni, Acoma, Laguna, Taos, and others) who share architectural and agricultural traditions but speak different languages from multiple language families. Using a single designation risks erasing their diversity. ETHICS-002: Juan de Onate's colonization (1598) was brutal — at Acoma Pueblo (1599), men over 25 had one foot amputated; women and children were enslaved. ETHICS-003: The Pueblo Revolt of 1680, led by Po'pay of Ohkay Owingeh, was the most successful indigenous uprising against European colonialism in North America, expelling the Spanish for 12 years. Pueblo peoples persist today across 19 pueblos in New Mexico and the Hopi reservation.

name_variants.name: Pueblo peoples
name_variants.context: Collective designation for diverse groups sharing architectural traditions
name_variants.source: Sando, J.S. Pueblo Nations (1992)

territory_changes.region: Rio Grande Valley / Four Corners region
territory_changes.description: Development of settled agricultural communities with distinctive multi-story adobe and stone architecture. Multiple language families represented: Tanoan, Keresan, Zuni (isolate), and Uto-Aztecan (Hopi).

Juan de Onate's colonization of New Mexico. Brutal suppression of resistance at Acoma Pueblo (1599): men over 25 had one foot amputated; women and children enslaved. Forced conversion and labor imposed on all pueblos.

Pueblo Revolt: coordinated uprising led by Po'pay of Ohkay Owingeh expels all Spanish colonists from New Mexico. The most successful indigenous uprising against European colonialism in North America. Pueblo independence maintained for 12 years until the Spanish reconquest of 1692.

sources.citation: Sando, J.S. Pueblo Nations: Eight Centuries of Pueblo Indian History (1992), Clear Light Publishers
Knaut, A.L. The Pueblo Revolt of 1680 (1995), University of Oklahoma Press
Preucel, R.W. Archaeologies of the Pueblo Revolt (2002), University of New Mexico Press

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Charrúa indigenous people, who originally inhabited the territory, were largely exterminated during the colonial and early republican periods. The Massacre of Salsipuedes (1831), ordered by President Fructuoso Rivera, killed most remaining Charrúa. Surviving Charrúa were distributed as servants, and four were taken to Paris to be exhibited as curiosities. ETHICS-003: Uruguay's national identity has historically been constructed as 'European' while erasing indigenous and African contributions. Independence was achieved as a buffer state between Argentina and Brazil, brokered by British diplomacy.

name_variants.name: Uruguay
name_variants.context: Common English short form
name_variants.source: Finch, H. A Political Economy of Uruguay Since 1870 (1981)

name_variants.name: Banda Oriental
name_variants.context: Colonial-era designation for the territory
name_variants.source: Barrán, J.P. Historia de la sensibilidad en el Uruguay (1989)

territory_changes.description: Treaty of Montevideo: Uruguay gains independence as a buffer state between Argentina and Brazil, brokered by British mediation. Preceded by José Gervasio Artigas's failed independence struggle and Brazilian annexation (1821-1828).
Massacre of Salsipuedes: President Fructuoso Rivera orders the killing and capture of the remaining Charrúa people — a deliberate act of ethnic cleansing. Survivors enslaved or exhibited in Europe.

sources.citation: Finch, H. A Political Economy of Uruguay Since 1870 (1981), St. Martin's Press
Acosta y Lara, E.F. La guerra de los Charrúas en la Banda Oriental (1961), Monteverde
Barrán, J.P. Historia de la sensibilidad en el Uruguay (1989), Ediciones de la Banda Oriental

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: Paraguay is notable for maintaining the Guarani language as a co-official national language alongside Spanish — a rare case of an indigenous language achieving official status. ETHICS-002: The War of the Triple Alliance (1864-1870) was one of the most devastating conflicts in modern history: Paraguay lost approximately 60-90% of its male population and large portions of its territory to the combined forces of Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay. This war is often overlooked in global historical narratives. Some scholars describe the destruction as genocidal.

name_variants.name: Paraguay
name_variants.context: Common English short form
name_variants.source: Whigham, T. The Paraguayan War (2002)

name_variants.name: Paraguái
name_variants.context: Guarani designation; Guarani is co-official language
name_variants.source: Mélia, B. La lengua guaraní del Paraguay (2010)

territory_changes.description: Paraguayan independence from Spain, achieved with minimal violence. Subsequently, José Gaspar Rodríguez de Francia established an isolationist dictatorship (1814-1840) that was remarkably self-sufficient.

End of the War of the Triple Alliance (1864-1870). Paraguay lost approximately 60-90% of its male population and significant territory to Argentina and Brazil. Proportionally one of the most devastating wars in modern history.

sources.citation: Whigham, T. The Paraguayan War, Vol. 1: Causes and Early Conduct (2002), University of Nebraska Press
Potthast, B. & Whigham, T. 'The Paraguayan Rosetta Stone,' Latin American Research Review 34(1) (1999)
Mélia, B. La lengua guaraní del Paraguay (2010), MAPFRE

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: Quilombo dos Palmares was the largest and longest-lasting community of escaped enslaved Africans (maroons) in the Americas, surviving for nearly a century. At its height, it had an estimated 20,000-30,000 inhabitants. It represents the most successful sustained resistance to the Atlantic slave trade system. Its leader Zumbi dos Palmares (d. 1695) is a national hero in Brazil; November 20 (the date of his death) is Black Consciousness Day. ETHICS-001: Palmares was not merely a refuge but a functioning political entity with its own governance, agriculture, and defense systems, drawing on both African and indigenous Brazilian traditions. Its destruction required repeated military campaigns by Dutch and Portuguese forces.

name_variants.name: Palmares
name_variants.context: Common short form in Portuguese
name_variants.source: Anderson, R.N. 'The Quilombo of Palmares,' JLAS 28(3) (1996)

name_variants.name: Republic of Palmares
name_variants.context: English designation sometimes used; 'republic' is debated
name_variants.source: Schwartz, S.B. Slaves, Peasants, and Rebels (1992)

name_variants.name: Angola Janga
name_variants.context: Name meaning 'Little Angola,' reflecting the Angolan origins of many inhabitants
name_variants.source: Funari, P.P.A. 'A arqueologia de Palmares,' Estudos Ibero-Americanos (1996)

territory_changes.description: Foundation of Palmares by enslaved Africans who escaped from Portuguese sugar plantations. The quilombo grew into a network of fortified settlements drawing on African political and agricultural traditions. Population grew to 20,000-30,000.

Final destruction by a force led by bandeirante Domingos Jorge Velho. The capital Macaco was besieged and destroyed. Zumbi escaped but was captured and killed in 1695; his head was displayed publicly in Recife as a warning to other enslaved people.

sources.citation: Anderson, R.N. 'The Quilombo of Palmares: A New Overview,' JLAS 28(3) (1996): 545-566
Schwartz, S.B. Slaves, Peasants, and Rebels (1992), University of Illinois Press
Funari, P.P.A. 'A arqueologia de Palmares,' Estudos Ibero-Americanos 22(2) (1996)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: The Guarani Jesuit Missions present a complex ethical case. On one hand, they protected Guarani peoples from the worst abuses of colonial slave-raiders (bandeirantes). On the other, they imposed European religious conversion and cultural transformation. The missions combined Guarani communal traditions with Jesuit organization. At their height, approximately 140,000 Guarani lived in 30 missions. The Guarani were armed to defend against slave raids — an unusual case of indigenous-European military cooperation. ETHICS-002: The expulsion of the Jesuits (1767) left the Guarani vulnerable to exploitation. The ruins are now UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay.

name_variants.name: Jesuit Missions of the Guaranis
name_variants.context: English academic designation
name_variants.source: Ganson, B. The Guarani Under Spanish Rule (2003)

name_variants.name: Reducciones Guaraníticas
name_variants.context: Spanish designation; 'reducción' meaning gathering dispersed populations into settlements
name_variants.source: Mörner, M. The Political and Economic Activities of the Jesuits in the La Plata Region (1953)

name_variants.name: Guarani Jesuit Republic
name_variants.context: Sometimes called a 'republic' though this overstates its political independence
name_variants.source: Cunninghame Graham, R.B. A Vanished Arcadia (1901)

territory_changes.description: Establishment of the first Jesuit reductions among the Guarani. The missions offered protection from Portuguese slave-raiders (bandeirantes) in exchange for religious conversion. Guarani communal traditions were partially preserved within the mission system.

territory_changes.region: Eastern Missions (present-day Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil)
territory_changes.description: War of the Seven Reductions: Guarani resist forced transfer to Spanish territory under the Treaty of Madrid (1750). Spanish and Portuguese forces jointly attack the missions. Over 1,500 Guarani killed at the Battle of Caibaté.

Expulsion of the Jesuits from Spanish territories by Charles III. Without Jesuit administration, the missions collapse. Guarani populations dispersed, many subjected to exploitation by colonial officials and settlers.

sources.citation: Ganson, B. The Guarani Under Spanish Rule in the Rio de la Plata (2003), Stanford University Press
Mörner, M. The Political and Economic Activities of the Jesuits in the La Plata Region (1953), Stockholm
Sarreal, J.M. The Guarani and Their Missions (2014), Stanford University Press

name_original: Reino de la Araucanía y Patagonia
ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: This 'kingdom' was declared by Orélie-Antoine de Tounens, a French lawyer, who claimed election by Mapuche chiefs. The claim is highly contested and may represent a European colonial fantasy. Some Mapuche leaders may have seen strategic advantage in a European alliance against Chile, while others never recognized Tounens. The episode risks trivializing both Mapuche sovereignty and the violence of Chilean/Argentine conquest. Tounens was arrested by Chile in 1862 and declared insane. ETHICS-001: This entity has no serious scholarly support as a legitimate state. It is included as an instructive example of colonial-era claims and contested sovereignty.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Araucania and Patagonia
name_variants.context: English designation; legitimacy is highly disputed
name_variants.source: Braun Menéndez, A. El reino de Araucanía y Patagonia (1945)

name_variants.name: Royaume d'Araucanie et de Patagonie
name_variants.context: French designation, as claimed by Orelie-Antoine de Tounens
name_variants.source: Braun Menendez, A. (1945)

territory_changes.description: Orélie-Antoine de Tounens declares himself King of Araucania and Patagonia, claiming support from Mapuche leaders. The legitimacy and extent of Mapuche consent is highly disputed.

Tounens arrested by Chilean authorities, declared insane, and deported to France. The 'kingdom' ceases to exist. Meanwhile, Chile began the Occupation of Araucanía (1861-1883), conquering Mapuche territory militarily.

sources.citation: Braun Menéndez, A. El reino de Araucanía y Patagonia (1945), Editorial Francisco de Aguirre
Navarro Floria, P. 'La conquista de la Patagonia y el reparto de tierras,' in Bandieri, S. (ed.) Cruzando la cordillera... (2001)
